criteria.name: Model Selection Appropriateness
criteria.1: Wrong model chosen (e.g., FCFF for a bank, DDM for a high-growth company with no dividends), no rationale provided
criteria.3: Reasonable model choice with basic rationale, model fits the company type, minor issues with justification
criteria.5: Optimal model selected with clear rationale tracing to company characteristics (capital structure stability, dividend policy, industry type), alternative models considered and reasons for rejection stated

criteria.name: Base Year Cash Flow Accuracy
criteria.1: Base year cash flows taken directly from financial statements without adjustment, key items missing (no working capital change, no tax adjustment), formula errors
criteria.3: Base year cash flows computed correctly from adjusted financials, tax rate applied, CapEx and depreciation identified, working capital change included
criteria.5: Base year cash flows meticulously derived from cleaned financials (R&D capitalized, leases converted, one-time items removed), all components sourced and documented, derived metrics (ROC, reinvestment rate, margin) computed and cross-checked against industry

criteria.name: Growth Rate Justification
criteria.1: Growth rate assumed without justification, inconsistent with fundamentals (high growth with no reinvestment), or contradicts narrative
criteria.3: Growth rate justified by at least one method (fundamental, historical, or consensus), generally consistent with reinvestment assumptions
criteria.5: Growth rate derived from fundamental method (g = reinvestment rate x ROC), cross-checked against historical and consensus, explicitly linked to business narrative (TAM, competitive position, life cycle), high-growth period length justified by competitive advantage assessment

criteria.name: Projection Consistency
criteria.1: Year-by-year projections contain internal contradictions (growth without reinvestment, margins that violate industry physics), missing years or incomplete table
criteria.3: Projections are internally consistent, growth and reinvestment aligned, margins converge toward reasonable target, all years populated
criteria.5: Projections show clear logical progression: revenue bounded by TAM, margins converging to target with explicit rationale, reinvestment rate consistent with growth and ROC, each input traceable to narrative assumption, transition to stable period smooth and justified

criteria.name: Terminal Value Reasonableness
criteria.1: Terminal value computed with stable growth exceeding GDP growth, no reinvestment adjustment in stable period, or formula applied incorrectly
criteria.3: Terminal value uses growing perpetuity with stable growth at or below risk-free rate, reinvestment rate adjusted for stable period, proportion of total value noted
criteria.5: Terminal value rigorously constructed: stable growth bounded by risk-free rate or GDP, reinvestment rate = g/ROC, cost of capital converged to mature levels, exit multiple cross-check performed, terminal value proportion flagged if extreme, sensitivity to stable growth shown

criteria.name: Discounting Correctness
criteria.1: Cash flows and discount rates mismatched (FCFF at cost of equity, FCFE at WACC), present value factors computed incorrectly, discounting convention unclear
criteria.3: Correct matching of cash flows to discount rates, present value factors correct, discounting convention stated
criteria.5: Discounting fully consistent (FCFF at WACC, FCFE at ke, DDM at ke), present value factors verified, convention stated (end-of-year or mid-year with rationale), multi-currency handled correctly if applicable, terminal value discounted at appropriate year

criteria.name: Equity Bridge Completeness
criteria.1: No bridge from firm value to equity (for FCFF), or bridge missing major components (debt not subtracted, cash not added, options ignored)
criteria.3: Bridge includes main components: debt subtracted, cash added, per-share value computed. Options may be handled via diluted share count
criteria.5: Complete equity bridge: market value of debt subtracted (not book value), cash and non-operating assets added, employee stock options valued (treasury stock or Black-Scholes) and subtracted, minority interests addressed, diluted share count used with method stated

criteria.name: Sensitivity Analysis
criteria.1: No sensitivity analysis, single point estimate only, no acknowledgment of uncertainty
criteria.3: Two-way sensitivity grid (growth vs. WACC) computed, base case identified, range of outcomes stated
criteria.5: Comprehensive sensitivity: two-way grid on growth and WACC, additional dimensions tested (margin, growth period length, reinvestment), tornado chart or ranked sensitivity identifying highest-impact inputs, value range clearly stated (bull/bear/base), breakeven analysis for key decision thresholds

criteria.name: Value Decomposition
criteria.1: No breakdown of where value comes from, terminal value proportion not stated
criteria.3: Terminal value proportion stated and flagged if high (>80%), basic split between near-term and terminal value shown
criteria.5: Full decomposition: value from current operations, value from high-growth period, value from terminal period each quantified, terminal value proportion assessed against norms (50-80%), cash and non-operating asset contribution identified, implications for sensitivity discussed

criteria.name: Overall Plausibility
criteria.1: Final value is implausible relative to market price, comparables, or common sense, with no acknowledgment of the divergence
criteria.3: Final value is reasonable, compared to current market price, basic margin of safety or implied growth analysis performed
criteria.5: Final value is plausible and contextualized: compared to market price with margin of safety, compared to relative valuation estimates, implied growth rate and ROIC reverse-engineered from market price, narrative consistency verified (value story matches numbers story), key risks to valuation thesis identified

key_requirements: Base year FCFF computed from adjusted financials with correct tax treatment
Growth rate derived from reinvestment rate x ROC, cross-checked with historical/consensus
Terminal value with stable growth at or below risk-free rate, reinvestment rate = g/ROC
Complete equity bridge: firm value - debt + cash - options = equity
Two-way sensitivity grid (growth vs WACC) with base case marked
common_pitfalls: Using book value of debt instead of market value in equity bridge
Forgetting to adjust reinvestment rate in stable period
Keeping high-growth WACC in terminal value when capital structure should converge
Omitting employee stock option value for tech companies

key_requirements: FCFE includes net debt issuance/repayment, not just operating items
Discount rate is cost of equity, not WACC
No equity bridge needed (result is equity value directly), but option deduction still required
Debt ratio assumption stated and justified as stable
Cross-check: FCFE model value should equal FCFF model value minus debt if assumptions are consistent
common_pitfalls: Discounting FCFE at WACC instead of cost of equity
Ignoring net debt flows (treating FCFE as FCFF)
Assuming debt ratio is stable when the company is actively deleveraging

key_requirements: Company has consistent dividend track record and payout ratio
Growth rate reflects retention ratio x ROE, not just historical dividend growth
Stable payout ratio justified (company is mature, not retaining for high-return projects)
If buybacks are significant, augmented DDM (dividends + buybacks) considered
common_pitfalls: Using DDM for a company that retains most earnings for high-ROE reinvestment
Ignoring buybacks that represent a significant portion of cash returns
Stable growth rate exceeding sustainable rate (retention x ROE)

key_requirements: Clear rationale for three stages rather than two (long growth runway, young company)
Transition period parameters interpolate linearly (growth, reinvestment, beta, debt ratio)
All three stages internally consistent (growth matches reinvestment at each stage)
Terminal value computed at end of transition period, not end of high-growth period
common_pitfalls: Transition period too short (1-2 years) making it effectively two-stage
Parameters not interpolating smoothly (abrupt changes between stages)
Cost of capital not converging during transition

key_requirements: Revenue-based approach with margin convergence to mature-industry target
Sales-to-capital ratio used for reinvestment estimation
Failure probability explicitly estimated and applied
Target margin sourced from mature industry peers, not management projections
Value = DCF x (1 - P_failure) + Distress value x P_failure
common_pitfalls: Using management's optimistic margin targets without industry benchmarking
Ignoring failure probability for early-stage companies
Applying standard reinvestment rate when sales-to-capital is more appropriate

common_failure_modes.symptom: FCFF discounted at cost of equity, or FCFE discounted at WACC. Result will be systematically biased (FCFF at ke overstates equity value, FCFE at WACC understates it)
common_failure_modes.detection: Check which cash flow is being discounted and verify the discount rate label matches (WACC for FCFF, ke for FCFE/DDM)
common_failure_modes.fix: Enforce the consistency principle: FCFF pairs with WACC, FCFE pairs with cost of equity, dividends pair with cost of equity

common_failure_modes.symptom: Terminal value is enormous because the denominator (r - g) is very small, pushing total value to implausible levels
common_failure_modes.detection: Compare stable growth rate to risk-free rate and nominal GDP growth. If g_stable > risk-free rate, the model is likely broken
common_failure_modes.fix: Cap stable growth at risk-free rate (conservative) or nominal GDP growth (upper bound). For most developed-market companies, 2-3% nominal is appropriate

common_failure_modes.symptom: Stable growth is 3% but reinvestment rate is 0% (or vice versa: 50% reinvestment with 2% growth). This implies value creation from nothing or massive value destruction
common_failure_modes.detection: Check that stable reinvestment rate = g_stable / ROC_stable. If ROC is not stated, this check cannot be performed
common_failure_modes.fix: Explicitly compute stable reinvestment rate as g/ROC. State the implied stable ROC and verify it is reasonable (typically converging toward cost of capital for mature companies)

common_failure_modes.symptom: FCFF model produces firm value but analyst reports per-share value without showing debt subtraction, cash addition, or option deduction. Creates an unexplained gap
common_failure_modes.detection: Check whether the bridge from firm value to per-share value shows each component
common_failure_modes.fix: Show complete bridge: firm value - market value of debt + cash + non-operating assets - option value - minority interests = equity value. Divide by diluted shares

common_failure_modes.symptom: Single point estimate presented as 'the value' without any range or acknowledgment of uncertainty. Creates false precision
common_failure_modes.detection: Check whether the output includes any variation of key inputs
common_failure_modes.fix: At minimum, produce a two-way grid varying stable growth rate and WACC. Report value as a range (e.g., $42-$58 per share, base case $49)

common_failure_modes.symptom: Almost all value comes from the terminal period, meaning the explicit projection period contributes very little. The valuation is effectively a single perpetuity
common_failure_modes.detection: Compute terminal value as a percentage of total firm/equity value
common_failure_modes.fix: Consider whether the high-growth period is too short, growth rate too low, or reinvestment too high during explicit period. Extending the projection or raising near-term growth may be appropriate if supported by the narrative

common_failure_modes.failure: Book value of debt used instead of market value
common_failure_modes.symptom: Equity value in the bridge uses book value of debt (from balance sheet) rather than market value, leading to systematic over- or under-estimation of equity
common_failure_modes.detection: Check the source of the debt figure subtracted in the equity bridge
common_failure_modes.fix: Use market value of debt. If not available, estimate by discounting future debt payments at current cost of debt. For investment-grade companies, book and market are usually close; for distressed companies, the difference is material

common_failure_modes.symptom: Equity value divided by basic shares, ignoring potentially large option grants. Per-share value is overstated, sometimes by 10-20% for tech companies
common_failure_modes.detection: Check whether options are addressed (either via diluted share count or separate option value subtraction)
common_failure_modes.fix: Use treasury stock method (add diluted shares) or Black-Scholes (subtract option value from equity, divide by basic shares). For companies with >5% of shares in options, this is material

common_failure_modes.symptom: Growth rate appears as a number with no link to business story (TAM, competitive position, life cycle). Cannot be challenged or updated because its basis is unclear
common_failure_modes.detection: Ask 'Why 12% and not 8% or 16%?' If no narrative answer exists, the input is not grounded
common_failure_modes.fix: Trace each input to the business narrative: revenue growth from TAM and share trajectory, margins from industry benchmarks and competitive position, reinvestment from capital intensity of the business model

common_failure_modes.symptom: High growth rate applied to income that already includes the reinvestment needed for that growth. This happens when growth is applied to operating income before subtracting reinvestment, then reinvestment is also subtracted
common_failure_modes.detection: Check that FCFF = After-tax EBIT x (1 - Reinvestment Rate), not After-tax EBIT x (1 + g) x (1 - Reinvestment Rate) applied twice
common_failure_modes.fix: Apply growth to after-tax operating income to get next year's income, then subtract reinvestment to get FCFF. The growth rate and reinvestment rate work together, not independently
